coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped quantum dots as high-efficiency nanoprobes for vanillin detection in complex samples and cell imaging applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianchun
Tian, Fengling
Meng, Feifei
Zhang, Lijuan
Jiang, Li
Tang, Shan
Lv, Naixia
Bao, Linchun
coredata.dc:description: A new type of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained by an economical, green, and feasible hydrothermal treatment using l-arginine and citric acid as carbon sources. The maximum value of the N-CDs fluorescence excitation/emission wavelength was 320/400 nm, showing an ultrahigh fluorescence quantum yield (51.7%). The fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively enhanced via vanillin, due to the combination of internal filtration effect and static polymerization. A fluorometric method was perfectly established for determining trace vanillin in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples with a wide linear range of 0.0152–15.2 μg/mL, accompanied by the limit of detection is 4.56 ng/mL. Satisfactory recoveries were found to be range from 90.4% to 117%, with a relative standard deviation in the range of 1.5–7.9%. The prepared N-CDs were successfully used for A549 cell imaging. These results indicate that the newly developed and high-efficient nanoprobes N-CDs have great application potential in vanillin detection and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped quantum dots
l-arginine
Citric acid
Vanillin
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Determination of vanillin in real samples
Possible mechanism of nitrogen-doped carbon dots examining vanillin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) High-resolution TEM image of the prepared N-CDs, (B) the size distribution (C), AFM image, and (D) height map of N-CDs.
(A) XPS survey of N-CDs, and high-resolution for: (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s.
(A) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of N-CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence of N-CDs; (C) Fluorescence stability of N-CDs; (D) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve about N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of the N-CDs toward vanillin (15.2 μg/mL) and other interference (100 μM), a. blank, b. vanillin, c. glucose, d. urea, e. l-lysine, f. ascorbic acid, g. ribonic acid, h.g-aminobutyric acid, i. Fe3+, j. Na+, k. K+, l. Ca2+, m. Fe2+, n. Mn2+, o. Cu2+, p. Mg2+, q. Zn2+; (B) Fluorescence of N-CDs before and after adding of 0–15.2 μg/mL vanillin; (C) Fluorescence intensities about N-CDs vs vanillin concentration plot for linear calibration.
(A) Fluorescence decay time of N-CDs alone and N-CDs with 10 μmol/L of vanillin. (B) UV–vis absorption of N-CDs, vanillin, and N-CDs + 10 μmol/L vanillin.
Bright field and fluorescent images about A549 cells with diversified concentrations of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 561 nm).
The fabrication process of the fluorescent sensor.
Vanillin detection in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped quantum dots as high-efficiency nanoprobes for vanillin detection in complex samples and cell imaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Minzu Normal University of Xingyi, Xingyi, 562400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Minzu Normal University of Xingyi, Xingyi, 562400, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Testing, Zhengzhou University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450044, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Analysis and Testing, Zhengzhou University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450044, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Clinical Laboratory, Qian Xi Nan People's Hospital, Xingyi, 562400, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Clinical Laboratory, Qian Xi Nan People's Hospital, Xingyi, 562400, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained by an economical, green, and feasible hydrothermal treatment using l-arginine and citric acid as carbon sources. The maximum value of the N-CDs fluorescence excitation/emission wavelength was 320/400 nm, showing an ultrahigh fluorescence quantum yield (51.7%). The fluorescence of N-CDs was effectively enhanced via vanillin, due to the combination of internal filtration effect and static polymerization. A fluorometric method was perfectly established for determining trace vanillin in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples with a wide linear range of 0.0152–15.2 μg/mL, accompanied by the limit of detection is 4.56 ng/mL. Satisfactory recoveries were found to be range from 90.4% to 117%, with a relative standard deviation in the range of 1.5–7.9%. The prepared N-CDs were successfully used for A549 cell imaging. These results indicate that the newly developed and high-efficient nanoprobes N-CDs have great application potential in vanillin detection and cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanillin (4-hydroxy-3-methoxy-benzaldehyde) is the major flavor component of natural vanilla, which is applied in a variety of food, chemical, pharmaceutical, and medical applications [1–3]. In the food industry, it is commonly found in spices used in beverages, candles, incenses, spices, perfume, air fresheners, and flavoring additives [4,5]. In the pharmaceutical industry, vanillin is an important intermediate for synthesizing antibacterial materials and drug precursors for l-dopa and methyldopa. Owing to its anti-inflammatory properties, vanillin plays a critical role in medical and clinical therapy [6]. Vanillin has been reported to have significant potential as an anticancer agent and an antioxidant and antibacterial agent in relieving chronic liver injury by inhibiting Il-6, TNF-α, and Il-1β [7]. Lee et al. emphasized the protective effect of vanillin on stem cells against ultraviolet radiation exposure [8]. Nevertheless, vanillin can cause headaches, nausea, and vomiting if ingested in large quantities and affect liver and kidney functions [9]. Therefore, it is imperative to implement a highly selective, sensitive, and simple treatment for testing vanillin and quantification.

Up to now, multifarious techniques have been established for detecting vanillin concentrations, including liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection and mass spectrometry [10–12], capillary electrophoresis [13], electrochemical sensing [14–17], surface enhanced Raman scattering [18], and fluorescence [9,19]. Among these methods, the fluorescent probe method has been widely investigated owing to its rapidity, simplicity, cost efficiency, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new type of carbon nanomaterials, which has paid much considerable attention since Xu et al. first developed them in 2004 [20]. CDs have become very promising in fluorescent sensor technologies [21–24], electro-analysis [25], biomedicine [25,26], drug delivery [27], and cell imaging [28], because of their unique properties, including benign aqueous solubility [29], ultrahigh photoluminescence [30], outstanding biocompatibility [31], and lower toxicity [32]. It has been widely accepted the hydrothermal methods of multifarious precursors, and is one of the most common methods because of its simplicity and lower cost. Nevertheless, most of the CDs obtained through this method present low quantum yields and limited active sites, hampering their broad application. Heteroatomic doping of CDs (nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur) is a practical strategy to solve this problem, which can be solved by modulating the intrinsic properties of CDs, and enhance the fluorescence properties and quantum yield [33].

This study employed a one-step hydrothermal treatment to prepare novel N-CDs based on l-arginine and citric acid. Comparison displayed the novel synthesized N-CDs revealed a high quantum yield (51.7%) upon introducing citric acid as a modifier. As shown in Scheme 1 , a fluorescent method was developed to monitor low concentration vanillin in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples with outstanding results, additionally, N-CDs were successfully applied for lung cancer cell (A549) imaging. According to our knowledge, it is the first study to employ newly synthesized N-CDs, on the basis of l-arginine and citric acid, for vanillin detection and A549 cell imaging.

Vanillin and l-arginine were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate dihydrate, citric acid, glucose, urea, ascorbic acid, ribonic acid, l-lysine, and g-aminobutyric acid were obtained with J&K Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). NaHCO3, KCl, CaCl2, Fe(NO3)3, FeSO4·7H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CuCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-Htetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) were bought from Suo Laibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). A549 cells were provided by Kejin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was acquired via mixing reserve fluid of 0.10 μmol/L NaH2PO4 and 0.10 μmol/L Na2HPO4, and above compounds were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was applied throughout this study. Unless otherwise stated, all reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was characterized by a BRUCKER D8 diffraction default instrument (Germany). Cell imaging was performed using a VIEW-VOX & IX-81 confocal laser-scanning microscope (CL-SM) (Tokyo, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using the Hitachi-F20 TEM (Tokyo, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was measured via the Bruker Multimode 8 AFM system (Karlsruhe, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic analysis was obtained by the FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Nicolet 6700, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Madison, USA). The cell activity was measured using a full-featured microplate detector (Shanghai, China). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum were observed by the Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence images were captured using SONY DSC W800 and ZF-20D handheld UV lamps at 320 nm (excitation wavelength). Confocal microimages were acquired on a BX53 fluorescence confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan).

N-CDs were prepared using an economical hydrothermal treatment. Principally, 0.50 g of l-arginine and 0.20 g of citric acid were added in 20 mL of ultra-pure water, subsequently, it's well mixed by ultrasonic oscillation for 20 min and transferred into the polyphenylene-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL), then heated at 220 °C with 12 h. The autoclave cooled naturally to 25 ± 10 °C after the reaction was terminated. The product was then centrifuged at 18,000 rpm with 20 min. The resultant brown supernatant was further filtered using the filtration membrane (0.22 μm) to remove impurities. Eventually, the filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C until further characterization and use.

Serum and urine samples (200 μL), which were drawn from patients with breast cancer, were acquired by Qian Xi Nan People's Hospital. All participants have been fully informed about the relevant procedures prior to obtaining written consent. The above samples were precisely measured, then added into 10 mL centrifuge tubes, following that were stored in polytetrafluoroethylene flasks at −20 °C until analysis.

Urine samples: Morning urine was collected in Teflon bottles. A 1.00 mL of urine sample was mixed with 5 mL acetonitrile and ultrasonicated for 10 min, whereafter, centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for another 5 min.

Serum samples: A 200 μL of serum sample and 5 mL acetonitrile were added into a 10 mL centrifuge tube and exposed to ultrasound for 5 min. The mixture was passed through the 0.22 μm membrane filter. The resulting samples were dried and dissolved in 1.0 mL ultra-pure water using ultrasound. Ultimately, the solution was filtered via the 0.22 μm membrane and further tested.

Biscuits and vanillin tablets were obtained from a local market and drugstore, respectively. One gram of each sample was ultrasonically extracted with 5 mL of water at 65 ± 10 °C for another 10 min. This procedure was repeated twice and the extracted solution was filtered with the 0.22 μm membrane for further use.

A 3.00 mL N-CD solution (300 μg/mL) was mixed by 200 μL vanillin of various concentrations (previously filtered with a syringe filter) in 10 mL centrifuge tubes. Then, each sample was mixed to 10 mL PBS solution (0.10 mmol/L, pH = 7.0). Subsequently, this buffer was fixed on 10 mL and shaken at 300 r/s on a shaker for 10 min. Eventually, it is obviously exhibited that the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at 320/400 nm.

A standard MTT method was applied to assess toxicity of N-CDs on A549 cells and this biolabeling potential of N-CDs was preliminarily assessed via imaging the A549 cells, more experimental details are provided in the Supporting Information (see Section 1).

The morphology of N-CDs was characterized by TEM and AFM, N-CDs display well-dispersed nanodots which are uniformly spherical and do not present any evident aggregation (Fig. 1 A). The high-resolution image of the N-CDs shows the measured average lattice distance about 0.18 nm, which is in accordance with the nanoparticles grow along the (0 0 2) face [34]. As Fig. 1B demonstrated, the N-CD size distribution is from 1 to 8 nm accompanied by an average size of 3.5 nm. The particle height distribution of N-CDs is observed by AFM and they do not show apparent aggregation (Fig. 1C). The average height of the N-CDs is 3.5 nm as Fig. 1D demonstrated, which is very close to the particle size of 3.5 nm measured by TEM. The XRD pattern of N-CDs shows a wide diffraction peak for at 2θ = 21.6° (Fig. S1(A)), this peak reveals amorphous carbon and the presence of planar spacing of graphite (0.18 nm), which confirms the high-resolution TEM results.

FT-IR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the surface groups and elemental composition about the N-CDs. Due to the small size of N-CDs, the diffraction peak is widened. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1(B)) shows that the absorption peak at 3416 cm−1 dedicates to the O–H [29] stretching peaks. In addition, the bond of C–H occurs at 2900 cm−1 [35]. These results prove the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs in the aqueous system. Then, characteristic peaks at approximately 1640 and 1427 cm−1 were contributed on the stretching vibrations of CO (derived from –COOH) [36] and C–N (nitrogen-containing) bonds [37], respectively, indicating that nitrogen has been faultlessly incorporated into CDs. Furthermore, these peaks about 1049 and 830 cm−1 were associated with stretching vibration of the C–O–C group [38] and the bending vibration of the N–H group [36], respectively. In addition, as depicted in Fig. S1(B), we compared the FT-IR spectrum of l-arginine alone and prepared N-CDs, and it was obvious from the images that most of the spectral peaks of N-CDs were similar to l-arginine. It is proved that N-CDs retains and inherits the structure of l-arginine [39]. The above peaks proved that the fluorescence intensity in N-CDs could be reduced by the photoinduced electron transfer process because the nitrogen atom in l-arginine had a pair of lone electrons, N-CDs retained and inherited the structure of l-arginine. This process proved to be a combination of internal filtration and static polymerization. Moreover, these peaks suggest that abundant functional bonds (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl) are present on this surface of the fluorescent N-CDs. Such groups can introduce various defects on the surface of the fluorescent N-CDs and act as excitation energy traps, and lead to unique optical properties. This presence of these bonds on the surface of fluorescent N-CDs induces excellent water solubility.

The elemental composition and chemical groups of N-CDs were assessed by XPS. The XPS results display there are three elements in N-CDs, including carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Three main absorption peaks in Fig. 2 A positioned at 286.0, 399.0, and 532.0 eV devoted to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, corresponding to the carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen content was calculated to be 70.97%, 12.02%, and 17.01%, respectively. The peak map of C 1s is shown in Fig. 2B. Four distinct peaks with binding energies about NC–N [40,41], C–O [30], and C–N [30] committed to 288.1, 286.2, and 285.2 eV, were observed in Fig. 2B. This result indicate that the surface of the synthesized N-CDs is abundant in hydrophilic groups, and it is in accordance with FT-IR spectrogram in Fig. S1(B). Furthermore, N 1s peaks on 400.0 and 401.5 eV (Fig. 2C) show that nitrogen is mainly present in the form of N–O [42] and –NH2 [19] groups, indicating that nitrogen moiety is doped into CDs. Finally, as seen in Fig. 2D, the O 1s spectrum was fitted two peaks, which corresponding to CO (531.4 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.7 eV) bonds [38]. The XPS surface composition measurements for N-CDs agree well with the FT-IR results. And that abundant functional groups present on their surface gradually enhance their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solutions, it is to be utmost importance about future applications of sensing and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Emission and absorption spectrums are crucial characterization tools about N-CDs, particularly applied to optical applications, such as fluorescence sensors, bio-imaging, and light-emitting devices. Therefore, the optical properties of N-CDs were further investigated, Fig. 3 A exhibited the diverse excitation wavelengths and emission wavelengths are mainly in 300–500 nm, when excitation wavelength of 320 nm, N-CDs displays highest emission intensity (400 nm). And that UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum about N-CD solution is displayed in Fig. 3B. N-CD solution exhibit absorption between 200 and 370 nm, on account of π–π* and n–π* transition about sp2 carbon and CO bond [43], respectively, corresponding to the abundance of chromophores on the surface of N-CDs. Typically, N-CDs presents a maximum emission peak at 400 nm upon excitation at 320 nm. N-CD aqueous solution under excitation with UV light at 320 nm, inset in Fig. 3B. Remarkably, under this fluorescence emission spectrum excited at 320 nm, a lightful blue fluorescence image could be monitored via the naked eye. When the N-CDs fluorescence is monitored over a wide range of wavelengths, N-CDs display excellent water solubility even without surface passivation. Additionally, Fig. 3C discusses the fluorescence stability about N-CDs in detail. This photostability about synthesized N-CDs was detected upon continuous excitation on 320 nm with 168 h. Above results indicate that under continuous UV exposure (without photobleaching), this fluorescence intensity about N-CD solution could maintain stable, confirming the excellent photostability about N-CDs. As you can see from Fig. 3D, time-correlated single-photon counting was contributed to performing fluorescence lifetime about N-CDs, the decay trajectory of N-CDs is fitted by from equation (1).
(1) y = A1 exp (-x/t1) + A2 exp (-x/t2) +A3 exp (-x/t3) + y0
where A1, A2, and A3 are the fractional contributions about the time-resolved decay lifetimes t1, t2, and t3, respectively, the average lifetime of N-CDs is 8.62 ± 0.05 ns (
χ
2 <1.2).

To further assess the optical properties about N-CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield was computed based on quinoline sulfate as a reference [44,45] (Supporting Information Section 3 and Fig. S2). The quantum yield was found to be 51.7% about N-CDs. For the sake of studying the applicability of N-CDs as a fluorescent biomarker in actual biological environment, this fluorescence stability about N-CD aqueous solution was assessed [46]. As revealed in Fig. S3 this is remarkable display that the fluorescence intensity increases with pH, reaches a peak for pH 5.0 to 9.0, and decreases with pH is ranging 10.0–13.0. This pH of the initial N-CD solution was approximately 10.0. Fig. S3 also displays fluorescence intensity about N-CDs, at a pH ranging from 5.0 to 9.0, is stronger, smoother, and steadier. It is well-known that the physiological environment has a pH between 5.0 and 9.0, which is beneficial for bioimaging applications. This fluorescence intensity about N-CDs decreases piece by piece, as pH about the solution increases because of the deprotonation of the N-CD surface, which reduces the net surface charge, resulting in the partial aggregation of N-CDs. Therefore, this aggregation about N-CDs further give rise to decreasing in fluorescence intensity. In summary, N-CDs has splendid optical stability under physiological conditions, it is adequately demonstrated its feasibility as nanoprobes.

Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the existence of various biomolecules and a few familiar metal ions at a concentration of 15.2 μg/mL, accompanied by an excitation/emission wavelength of 320/400 nm. These biomolecules used in this experiment included vanillin, glucose, urea, l-lysine, ascorbic acid, ribonic acid, and g-aminobutyric acid along with some common metal ions, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, and Zn2+. When the interferent is added to vanillin solution, there is no significant change between standard N-CDs containing vanillin and standard N-CDs containing interferon. These results clearly indicate that the N-CDs is highly specific and anti-interference for vanillin analysis. This mechanism of fluorescence enhancement can be explained by the oxidation about N-CDs in subsistence of vanillin and further decrease in photoinduced electron transfer of N-CDs. It is well-known that the photoinduced electron transfer process decreases the fluorescence intensity in the N-CDs owing to the nitrogen atom in l-arginine has a lone pair of electrons [39]. After the addition of vanillin, N-CDs undergo oxidation, and the lone pair of electrons in l-arginine decreases. Therefore, the amount of photoinduced electron transfer reduces, and fluorescence intensity about N-CDs enhances. Moreover, this process proves to be a combination of internal filtration and static polymerization. Subsequently, N-CDs were present in the oxidized form after the addition of vanillin [39].

Based on the above observations, various concentrations of vanillin (0–15.2 μg/mL) were added to the N-CD solution. This roughly assess the sensitivity about N-CD nanoprobes to vanillin under optimized conditions (pH = 7.0). The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4B) were recorded for these samples under optimized experimental and instrumental conditions. Fluorescence intensity gradually increases with increasing vanillin concentration. The corresponding calibration curve is plotted in Fig. 4C. The linear range for vanillin measurement was 0.0152–15.2 μg/mL (0.10–100 μM) and a linear regression coefficient is 0.999, the limit of detection (LOD) is 4.56 ng/mL (0.03 μM) on the basis of 3 S/N. The sensing probe synthesized in this study is the most promising tool for the sensitive determination of vanillin. It can find great applications in real sample analysis using serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of vanillin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A standard addition method that minimizes the matrix-interfering components was developed to determine the vanillin content in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples. We selected spiked serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples as models for the validation of our treatment using the standard addition method. Subsequently, the sample fluorescence fluorogram were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and the emission intensity was monitored at a wavelength of 400 nm. The results of triplicate experiments are listed in Table 1 . Trace vanillin in biscuit and vanillin tablet samples were detected at concentrations of 2.83 and 3.15 μg/g, respectively. These outstanding recoveries were ranging of 90.4–117%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 7.9%. The proposal method was compared with previous method of N-CDs for the measurement of vanillin [19,47–49]. As listed in Table S1, the probe method showed an excellent performance, including a high sensitivity, wide linear range, and excellent correlation coefficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of nitrogen-doped carbon dots examining vanillin
ce:section.ce:para: It is significant to explore the enhancing mechanism between N-CDs and vanillin. To further investigate the enhancing mechanism of vanillin for the fluorescence of N-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs and N-CDs + 10 μmol/L vanillin were inquired, respectively, their average lifetime were 8.62 ns and 7.80 ns, respectively (Fig. 5 A). The lifetime of N-CDs + 10 μmol/L vanillin is remarkable decreasing, the result indicated that a dynamic enhancing model might occur, it can be deduced that fluorescence enhancing is due to the dynamic mode since static enhancing does not decreasing the lifetime. Additionally, it can be seen from Fig. 5B, the UV–vis spectrums of N-CDs, 10 μmol/L vanillin, and N-CDs + 10 μmol/L vanillin were provided, the UV–vis absorption of N-CDs + 10 μmol/L vanillin generated a new peak at 350 nm along with UV spectroscopy changes, the enhancing course showed the vanillin might interact with N-CDs to form new substances, which has more π-π conjugate systems, giving rise to the fluorescence enhancing of N-CDs [19,46].

There is that the cytotoxicity of the materials was first assessed, in order to study the applicability about probes toward biological systems. As can be seen from Fig. S4, the MTT assay was performed at various concentrations (0–500 μg/mL) of N-CDs. Cell viability was greater than 89% after cultivation with 500 μg/mL about N-CDs for another 24 h. The high cell activity proved N-CDs had lower toxicity and splendid biocompatibility. This result confirms the future applicability of N-CDs as effective intracellular probes.

To explore the potential use about N-CDs as bioimaging probes and visualize the internalization of N-CDs in A549, confocal fluorescence microscopy (CFM) was applied on live cell imaging (Fig. 6 ). A549 cells were cultivated on a medium with 0, 25, and 125 μg/mL N-CDs for 30 min at 37 °C and examined under a CFM. Bright field images obtained after incubation indicate cell viability. During the transition time with 30 min, we acquired a very good interaction between N-CDs and A549 cells, and gained fluorescence images, manifesting N-CDs had outstanding membrane permeability. As expected, when the concentration of N-CDs is 0 μg/mL, the A549 cells incubated presented almost no fluorescence signal in all the three channels. At 405, 488, and 561 nm, the signals were fairly weak, which was similar to in vitro experiments. It is interesting when the concentration of N-CDs is 25 μg/mL, the fluorescence was observed in all the three channels, and when the concentration of N-CDs is 125 μg/mL, the fluorescence of A549 cells intensity was brighter in all the three channels. Moreover, the cells images of A549 are most effective at 488 nm, thus, we can conclude that the green approach to synthesize N-CDs is passably safe and has extensive applications in field of in vivo cell imaging. In brief, it is appropriate for imaging living cells because of their lightful fluorescence characteristics.

In short, a super-efficient and novel nanoprobe based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were successfully prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method with l-arginine and citric acid as raw materials. The N-CDs exhibited unique photoluminescence characteristics such as ultra-high quantum yield monochromatic emission, narrow excitation wavelength and good stability. Notably, the N-CDs presented excellent selectivity and sensitivity to vanillin. N-CDs were used to establish a highly sensitive detection platform for vanillin in serum, urine, biscuits, and vanillin tablet samples. In addition, N-CDs were successfully applied on cell imaging. In summary, we predict that fluorescent N-CDs could find wide applications in the field of biological imaging.

Qianchun Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, writing - review, and editing. Fengling Tian: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Feifei Meng: Investigation, Software, writing–original draft, writing–review and editing, Formal analysis. Lijuan Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, and writing the original draft. Li Jiang: Methodology, Project administration, writing–review, and editing. Shan Tang: Resources, writing review, and editing. Naixia Lv: writing - review, and editing. Linchun Bao: Investigation, writing–review, and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505115), the Top Scientific and Technological Talents in Universities of Guizhou Province (KY2018078), the Guizhou basic research project (ZK[2022]561), the Science and Technology for Youth Talent Growth Project of the Guizhou Provincial Education Department (KY2020217), the Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan (2021-2-31), and the Projects of Minzu Normal University of Xingyi (21XYZJ01 and 21XYZD02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2022.112743.
